title: Who Is Nelson Chauke? The Story Behind Nelson Chauke Properties
author: Tintswalo Chauke
excerpt: A factual look into the origins of Nelson Chauke Properties, the man behind the agency, and the verifiable public information surrounding his career in real estate.
content:
The Origins of Nelson Chauke Properties

According to public company records, Nelson Chauke Properties was officially registered as a private company on 20 October 2020. The registration lists Nelson Chauke as the director, placing him formally at the head of the business from day one. Since then, the agency has been active on major South African property platforms, marketing residential and commercial properties across Pretoria and nearby suburbs.

An Active Presence in the Pretoria Market

The agency maintains several live listings on well-known portals such as PropertyCentral. These listings include flats, houses and small commercial opportunities, mostly in Pretoria East and surrounding areas. This shows that the agency is not dormant – it operates openly under the founder’s name and maintains public-facing activity on both listing platforms and social media.

The business also runs its own Facebook page, sharing property posts and promotional material. An Instagram post from 2020 introduces Nelson Chauke as a Pretoria East Sales Agent with over 20 years of sales experience, reinforcing his presence in the real-estate space long before registering his own company.

The Rawson Question

While it is known and often stated online that he has industry experience, current publicly available digital records do not show a formal employment history at Rawson Property Group. Rawson’s Pretoria East office lists many agents, but his name does not appear in their current or indexed agent directories. Likewise, LinkedIn profiles and PPRA-linked data do not show a recorded Rawson role under his name. What can be confirmed is that he has operated in the same Pretoria East property market where Rawson also has a strong presence.

What the Verified Information Tells Us

  • He founded his own registered agency in 2020.
  • The agency is active on property portals, with multiple listings published publicly.
  • He maintains social-media presence for marketing and client outreach.
  • He is positioned as an experienced Pretoria East property professional, based on public posts and marketing material.

At this point, the publicly verifiable picture is clear: Nelson Chauke is the director and active operator behind a legitimate Pretoria-based estate agency, with several years of visible activity in the real-estate market. His company continues to operate independently, with its own brand, listings and client-facing communication channels.

title: First-time Buyers in South Africa: FLISP, Houses vs Apartments and What Actually Helps
author: Khushulwayo Sibanyoni
excerpt: A clear, no-nonsense guide for first-time buyers in South Africa: who qualifies for the First Home Finance subsidy (FLISP), the trade-offs between houses and apartments, and the exact steps that actually move the needle.
content:
Short, blunt summary

If you’re buying your first property and your household income sits between R3,501 and R22,000 a month, you may qualify for the government’s First Home Finance subsidy (formerly FLISP). It’s a once-off subsidy that can act as a deposit top-up or reduce your bond, lowering monthly repayments. You will typically need bond pre-approval before you apply.

Houses versus apartments — the practical choice

Buy a house if you want long-term stability, space and control. Houses can offer stronger capital-growth potential in the right suburbs, but expect higher maintenance and initial costs. Buy an apartment if you want lower entry costs, easier security and often faster rental demand — especially close to universities and business nodes. For many first-timers, apartments let you get onto the ladder sooner and begin building equity earlier, but always run the numbers.

How First Home Finance (FLISP) actually helps — the facts

  • Income band: targets households earning between R3,501 and R22,000 per month.
  • Subsidy size: income-tested; published guides show subsidy amounts ranging from tens of thousands up to roughly R170,000, depending on income band and household circumstances.
  • When to apply: you normally apply after you have home-loan approval in principle; the NHFC portal requires proof of finance as part of the application.
  • Uses: the subsidy can be used towards a deposit, to reduce the bond or to help with transfer/attorney fees in some cases.

Common misconceptions — corrected

Myth: FLISP is instant cash before you start. Not true. The subsidy is paid after you qualify and meet closing conditions; get bond pre-approval first. Myth: Apartments have no hidden costs. False — levies, special levies and insurance can materially affect affordability.

Concrete steps to take — no fluff

  1. Check eligibility: confirm household income, citizenship/permanent residency and first-time beneficiary rules.
  2. Get bond pre-approval: approach a bank or bond originator for approval in principle.
  3. Apply via the official portal: use the First Home Finance / NHFC portal to submit your application and subsidy estimate.
  4. Model full costs: include bond repayment, rates and taxes, sectional levies (if applicable), insurance and a maintenance buffer.
  5. Choose property type after cashflow modelling: include possible special levies and vacancy risk.
  6. Use a reputable conveyancer: get quotes for transfer and bond registration before signing offers.

Simple affordability checklist

Bond pre-approval in writing; First Home Finance estimate or application started; proof of ID and income; spreadsheet with monthly cost breakdown including levies and maintenance; conveyancer quotes for transfer and registration.

Parting shot

The subsidy is real and moves the needle for many first-time buyers on modest incomes — but it’s not a magic ticket. Do the maths, get pre-approval and apply through the official First Home Finance channels. That combination flips a dream into an affordable, sustainable home.

title: Property Investments in South Africa: Getting Started — A No-Nonsense Playbook
author: Tintswalo Chauke
excerpt: A practical, tight playbook for beginners: get bond pre-approval, run the three numbers that matter, pick the right micro-market and budget for every hidden cost before you buy.
content:
Quick, honest opening

Property investing is not glamour. It is cashflow, leverage, risk management and location. If you want to start small and scale, sort your finance first, build an emergency buffer, and pick a micro market that matches your strategy. Do the numbers before you fall in love with a building.

Basic rules that actually matter

Know what you want the property to do for you. Buy to let for immediate income or buy for value for capital appreciation. Your strategy decides suburb, size and measures of success. Start with bond pre-approval, not the listings. Pre-approval shows lenders what you can afford and gives negotiating power.

The three numbers you must run, every time

  • Gross yield: annual rent ÷ purchase price × 100.
  • Net yield: gross yield minus rates, levies, insurance, maintenance and management fees.
  • Loan servicing: bond repayment vs rental income plus your cashflow cushion.

Ignore headlines promising 20 percent yields. Realistic yields in many South African suburbs sit in the single digits to low double digits. Use conservative assumptions for vacancies and maintenance.

Location thinking that pays off

Macro matters, micro matters more. Pick a city with jobs, students, transport or infrastructure. Then drill down to suburbs with low vacancy and sensible price points. Filter shortlist using vacancy rate, affordability band and tenant profile.

What you must budget for beyond the purchase price

  • Transfer and bond registration fees.
  • Rates and municipal charges, which often rise faster than inflation.
  • Sectional title levies and special levies contingency.
  • Insurance, maintenance and letting agent fees.
  • Void period buffer — if you cannot handle six to eight weeks without rental income, do not buy to let yet.

Practical checklist before you sign anything

  1. Get bond pre-approval in writing.
  2. Run a five-year cashflow model including levy escalations and a 6–12 month maintenance reserve.
  3. Inspect body corporate minutes for sectional-title units and check for special levy risks.
  4. Speak to at least one local letting agent about rental and vacancy expectations.
  5. Get a conveyancer quote and confirm transfer timelines and likely costs.

Real expectations on returns and risks

Expect modest but steady rental growth. House-price growth can be muted in the short term, so treat property as a multi-year play. Use current market indices and local agent data rather than sensational headlines.

How to structure your first deal so it is repeatable

Leverage responsibly. Start with a buffer that lets you carry the bond for at least six months without rental income. Professionalise early. Use a managing agent if you cannot be local. Think exit as you buy. Choose properties that are easy to re-let or sell.

Simple tactics that actually work

  • Buy smaller units in high-demand suburbs to limit upfront capital and speed letting.
  • Buy for a clear tenant type for faster letting.
  • Negotiate transfer contributions from sellers where possible.
  • Reinvest rental surpluses into saving for the next deposit.

Final, blunt advice

Property is a long game. Get bond pre-approval, run numbers including levies, build a cash buffer and use current market data. Repeat this process confidently and scale sensibly.

title: Best Places to Start Property Investing in Pretoria (2025 snapshot)
author: Tintswalo Chauke
excerpt: A 2025 snapshot of Pretoria’s best starter suburbs for investors: practical micro-market choices, tenant profiles, the numbers to run and the precise due diligence to avoid costly mistakes.
content:
Quick, sharp opening

Pretoria is one of South Africa’s most practical places to start a property portfolio in 2025. It combines relatively affordable entry prices, diverse tenant pools and pockets of new development that keep demand steady. If you want realistic cashflow and repeatable scale, choose suburbs that match your strategy and run the numbers hard.

Why Pretoria — the 2025 snapshot

Pretoria offers above-average rental opportunity and sensible entry points because many sales sit in affordable bands and rental demand in key micro-markets remains strong. That lets investors spot properties with reasonable yields and capital-growth potential when chosen carefully.

Top suburbs to start in 2025

  • Menlyn (Menlyn Maine): mixed-use precinct, high demand from young professionals and modern apartment stock.
  • Hatfield: student market close to the University of Pretoria — steady demand but higher turnover.
  • Brooklyn and Lynnwood: near business nodes; attractive to professionals seeking quality finishes and security.
  • Garsfontein & Faerie Glen: family markets with longer leases and stability.
  • Centurion: value and scale between Pretoria and Midrand; good for family rentals and townhouses.

Area-by-area practical checks

Menlyn / Menlyn Maine

Watch developer phases and local supply. Tenants expect fibre, inverter and security. Check sectional budgets and levy history.

Hatfield

Verify university residence capacity and student intake. Decide furnished versus unfurnished depending on yield and capex appetite.

Brooklyn & Lynnwood

Expect premium rents and lower vacancy. Compare yield to suburbs with cheaper entry prices to ensure acceptable net returns.

Garsfontein & Faerie Glen

Family suburbs with stability. Check school zones and maintenance histories on freestanding homes.

Centurion

Look for transport upgrades and estate governance. Centurion offers lower purchase prices per unit and good scale for repeat purchases.

The exact numbers you must run

No emotion. Numbers only.

  • Gross yield: annual rent ÷ purchase price × 100.
  • Net yield: gross yield minus rates, levies, insurance, maintenance and management fees.
  • Loan servicing test: bond repayment at current rate plus a 2 percentage-point stress, a 6–8 week vacancy and a one-off special levy.

Practical checklist — non-negotiables

  1. Get bond pre-approval in writing.
  2. Run a 24–60 month cashflow model with stress tests.
  3. Inspect body corporate minutes for sectional title purchases.
  4. Talk to local letting agents for realistic rent and vacancy figures.
  5. Get a conveyancer quote and verify transfer timelines and costs.

Small upgrades that reduce voids

  • Install fibre-ready infrastructure.
  • Provide basic inverter/solar for lights and essentials.
  • Improve security and entrance appeal.
  • Use professional photos for listings.

Final take

Pretoria is a pragmatic market for disciplined investors. Pick one micro-market, learn it, run conservative forecasts and professionalise management early. Do those things and your Pretoria buy-to-let has a real chance of being repeatable and profitable.

title: Growing Property Investments: How to Scale Your Portfolio Without Getting Burned
author: Khushulwayo Sibanyoni
excerpt: A practical scaling playbook for beginner to medium investors: stabilise assets, access equity sensibly, professionalise operations and avoid the common traps that wipe out returns.
content:
Stop treating property like casino money

Scaling a property portfolio is not about luck. It is discipline, repeatable processes and conservative maths. Get one asset stable, understand where the cash comes from and goes to, and then scale using refinancing, conservative leverage and professional management. Rush it and you lose sleep and capital.

The five pillars of safe scaling

  1. Stabilise your first property: ensure predictable net rental income for six to 12 months.
  2. Leverage equity, not emotion: refinance or readvance sensibly; banks perform affordability checks.
  3. Keep debt serviceable under stress: test higher rates, a six to 12-week vacancy and higher levies.
  4. Professionalise property management early: good agents reduce vacancy and legal risk.
  5. Know your tax and exit rules: CGT and tax on rental income must be modelled into returns.

A repeatable scaling playbook

  1. Consolidate first: operate the first property for 6–12 months and create a maintenance and tenancy history.
  2. Stress test the numbers: build a 36-month cashflow model with rate shocks and vacancy allowances.
  3. Refinance or readvance to free capital: use bank products conservatively and account for costs.
  4. Purchase the next asset with conservative leverage: avoid maximum gearing on early deals.
  5. Professionalise systems: set KPIs for agents, track time-to-let and repair turnaround.
  6. Reinvest surpluses into the next deposit: avoid lifestyle creep.

How to use refinancing without being reckless

Understand readvance, further advances and full refinancing. Each option has costs and requires fresh affordability checks. Use only equity that keeps buffers intact and factor in bond re-registration costs and timing.

Portfolio shape that smooths cashflow

Blend high gross-yield assets — student accommodation and small sectional units — with family houses that offer stability. This mix smooths income and reduces vacancy risk across economic cycles.

Numbers you must run

  • Gross yield: annual rent ÷ purchase price × 100.
  • Net yield: gross yield minus all running costs including vacancy provision.
  • Loan serviceability test: repayment at current rate plus 2 percentage points, vacancy stress and a one-off special levy.
  • Five-year projection: conservative rental increases not best-case scenarios.

Taxes and admin — do not ignore it

Declare rental income, claim allowable expenses and plan for capital gains tax on disposal. Get an accountant who understands property early — poor tax planning can wipe out a year’s profit faster than a bad tenant.

Managing agents — why they matter

Agents provide tenant screening, arrears management, repairs co-ordination and reporting. Choose agents with references, clear KPIs and transparent fee structures.

Small upgrades with big impact

  • Install a basic inverter or solar lighting.
  • Ensure fibre-ready infrastructure.
  • Improve security and entrance appeal.
  • Use professional photography for listings.

Common traps

  • Overleverage on your first deal.
  • Ignore body corporate minutes and reserve funds.
  • Poor tenant screening and weak contract enforcement.
  • No buffer for rising rates and municipal charges.

Final practical counsel

Stabilise, stress test, access equity conservatively, professionalise operations and model taxes and exit costs. Build a balanced portfolio mix and use agents and accountants to remove operational risk. Do this and you can scale steadily without getting burned.

title: Finance and Risk When Growing Property Investments: Lending, Yields and What Lenders Look For
author: Khushulwayo Sibanyoni
imageHint: modern sectional title complex exterior at dusk
excerpt: A practical, no-nonsense playbook for investors scaling property in South Africa. What lenders check, how to underwrite yields conservatively and the controls that prevent a single shock from wrecking your returns.
content:
If you want to scale, the numbers must be iron-clad

Property investing is a numbers game

Lenders want proof, not promises

Banks lend on demonstrated serviceability, documented income and conservative stress tests. Promises of future rental increases or hoped-for capital gain will not substitute for real cash flow on paper. If your affordability collapses under a modest rate shock or a short vacancy, lenders will decline or limit the advance.

Key checks lenders run

  • Income and employment: payslips, IRP5s, tax returns for self-employment.
  • Monthly obligations: all debt repayments, levies, school fees and reasonable running-cost allowances.
  • Credit record: defaults, judgements and payment behaviour matter.
  • Security and marketability: location, municipal compliance and rental demand for that specific type of unit.
  • Stress testing: affordability at higher rates and conservative rental assumptions.

Common finance routes — choose the right tool

There are sensible and reckless ways to fund a portfolio. Use the right product for the task.

  • Standard bond: long-term mortgage secured over the property. Lowest cost of capital and the normal route for buy-to-let.
  • Refinance and equity release: revalue or refinance to unlock capital for the next deposit. Powerful but increases portfolio leverage.
  • Bridging and short-term developer finance: expensive and short-dated. Use only for clearly time-bound work such as renovations or construction gaps with an exit already arranged.
  • Joint ventures / seller finance: useful in thin-deposit situations but trade off returns and control. Always document exit mechanics.

Lending mechanics you must understand

Do not assume lenders operate the same way you do. Practical mechanics matter.

  • Loan to value: expect typical LTVs to be below 100 per cent. Many lenders require a deposit and will price investment bonds differently to owner-occupied loans.
  • Affordability models: lenders apply ratios and repayment stress tests; the old rule of thumb about 30 per cent of gross still surfaces but underwriters are more forensic now.
  • Security is necessary but not sufficient: lenders prefer demonstrated ability to service debt. Strong bricks do not override weak cash flow or a bad credit record.

Rental yields — practical numbers and how to model them

Yield is the first structural test of a buy-to-let deal. Know the difference between gross yield and net yield and underwrite conservatively.

How to calculate and stress yields

  1. Gross yield = annual rent ÷ purchase price × 100.
  2. Net yield = annual rent minus operating costs, vacancy allowance, management fees and insurance, all ÷ purchase price × 100.
  3. Stress tests: add 1–3 months vacancy, increase maintenance by 10–20% and run repayments at prime plus 2–3 percentage points or at the lender's stress rate.

Practical rule: if the market quote says 9 per cent gross, underwrite at 6.5–7 per cent gross until you have a local track record. Suburb-level data beats national aggregates every time.

The spreadsheet that passes — what lenders want to see

When you present a deal, your model must answer three simple questions clearly: can you service the loan without relying on best-case scenarios, can you absorb a typical shock, and do your assumptions have evidence?

Minimum elements for the lender pack

  • Purchase price, deposit, required bond amount and term.
  • Three recent comparable rents from actual lets, not agent optimism.
  • All running costs: municipal rates, body corporate levies, insurance, property management fees, and a maintenance allowance.
  • Vacancy allowance — at least one month per year for suburban stock; more for purpose-built student accommodation or specialised assets.
  • Stress scenarios — rates +200–300 basis points, rent down 10 per cent, one longer vacancy of three months.

Show the bank you can pay the bond without relying on future capital growth. If your plan needs capital appreciation to be serviceable, rethink the deal.

Controls that keep the portfolio alive

You can control many risks, and the difference between a well-run portfolio and one that fails is operational discipline.

  1. Cash buffer: target three to six months of portfolio expenses at a minimum. For highly leveraged portfolios or where you depend on rental income personally, aim for six to 12 months.
  2. Insurance: building and public liability cover are essential; consider rent loss or rent guarantee products for critical units.
  3. Tenant screening and legal backing: reference checks, credit screening and a robust lease reduce arrears and eviction costs.
  4. Preventative maintenance: schedule regular checks. Reactive repairs are more expensive and disruptive.
  5. Portfolio diversification: avoid putting all your units in one complex. Mix suburbs and tenancy profiles to reduce correlated risk.
  6. Debt governance: set internal caps for maximum portfolio LTV and maximum monthly bond serviceability as a percent of household income.

Tax, ownership structures and lending behaviour

Tax matters and how you hold assets affects lending outcomes.

  • Juristic entities: banks treat companies, trusts and close corporations differently. Expect stricter underwriting and sometimes higher deposit requirements when buying through an entity.
  • Tax deductions: allowable expenses reduce taxable income but will not convince a bank if your cash flow does not stack up. Use tax benefits to optimise returns, not to prove short-term affordability.

Get a property-savvy accountant early. Poor tax planning can wipe out a year’s profit faster than a bad tenant.

Scaling with discipline — a four-step playbook

  1. Validate the micro market: three comparables, vacancy rates, typical time-on-market and tenant profile.
  2. Underwrite conservatively: model lower rents and higher costs than the market sales pitch; stress test with higher rates.
  3. Protect cash flow: reserves, insurance and strict tenant screening. Treat rental income as operating revenue, not disposable income.
  4. Use equity intelligently: refinance only when the budget remains robust after added debt and costs. Equity release is a lever, not free money.

Worked example — be conservative with the numbers

Buy a sectional title flat for R1 200 000 with a 20 per cent deposit (R240 000) and expected market rent of R10 000 per month.

Gross yield = (R10 000 × 12) ÷ R1 200 000 = 10 per cent. That looks attractive. Now strip out the running costs: rates circa R1 000 per month, body corporate R1 500, insurance R200, management fees 10 per cent of rent R1 000, repairs allowance R500 and a vacancy allowance equal to one month per year R833. Annual operating costs ≈ R52 000. Net rent = R120 000 − R52 000 = R68 000. Net yield = R68 000 ÷ R1 200 000 ≈ 5.7 per cent.

Assume a bond of R960 000 over 20 years at 11 per cent. Monthly repayment ≈ R9 900. The rent is R10 000 — dangerously tight when you still have the running costs to cover. If rates rise or you get an extended vacancy, you will need to fund the shortfall personally. Aim for a healthy buffer between net income and debt service of at least 10–15 per cent on conservative assumptions.

Common mistakes and how to fix them

Mistake 1 — buying on future capital growth: lenders and most sensible investors underwrite today’s cash flows. If your strategy depends on appreciation to service debt, you are building on hope.

Fix: buy where rental yields make sense on today's numbers and treat growth as upside.

Mistake 2 — underestimating vacancy and legal costs: eviction processes are slow and expensive; many landlords are surprised by how long a problem tenant costs.

Fix: model legal costs, include a vacancy buffer and consider rent guarantee products on critical units.

Mistake 3 — overusing bridging finance: short-term debt turned into long-term exposure is a common trap when exits are delayed.

Fix: use bridging finance only with a signed exit or refinance plan and a contingency buffer.

Final checklist before you sign

  • Do you have documented comparables for rent, not guesses?
  • Has your spreadsheet been stress tested for higher rates and a rental shortfall?
  • Do you have two to six months of portfolio costs as a reserve?
  • Are debt limits and exit rules documented for each property?
  • Have you checked how a bank treats purchases done through a trust or company?

Bottom line

Property in South Africa can deliver strong returns, but only when you treat it like a business. Be conservative on underwriting, keep proper buffers, insure the big risks, and professionalise operations early. Lenders will probe your ability to pay long before they worry about market appreciation. Build discipline into your process and scaling becomes repeatable rather than risky.

Practical offer

If you want, paste one deal here — purchase price, deposit, expected rent and running costs — and I will run a conservative two-scenario model for you. No sales pitch, just numbers you can take to a bank or adviser.
